name: Sohrab
description: Sohrab is a tragic hero in Persian mythology, primarily known from Ferdowsi's epic poem, the Shahnameh (Book of Kings). He is the son of Rostam, the most celebrated hero of Persia, and Tahmineh, a princess from Turan. Sohrab's story is marked by themes of duty, fate, and the devastating consequences of ignorance and war. He grows up unaware of his father's identity, and Rostam is similarly unaware of his son. Sohrab is depicted as a formidable warrior, possessing immense strength and skill, eager to prove himself and establish his own kingdom. His ambition and martial prowess lead him into conflict with the Persian army, culminating in a fateful duel with his own father. The tragic irony of their unknowingly fighting each other forms the core of his narrative, highlighting the futility and sorrow inherent in war and the devastating impact of missed connections and mistaken identities. Sohrab embodies the qualities of a noble warrior, but his story serves as a cautionary tale about the destructive power of ignorance and the profound loss that can result from familial separation and conflict.
subtitle: The Tragic Warrior

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Sohrab
keyMyths.description: Rostam, while on a hunting trip in Turan, meets and falls in love with Princess Tahmineh. After a night together, Rostam leaves, giving Tahmineh an armband to give to their child, should it be a boy. He instructs her to tie it to the child's arm as a sign of his lineage. Tahmineh gives birth to Sohrab, concealing Rostam's identity from him, fearing that Rostam would take him away. This sets the stage for the tragic events that unfold later in Sohrab's life.

keyMyths.title: Sohrab's Challenge
keyMyths.description: Driven by ambition and a desire to prove himself, Sohrab decides to invade Persia. He believes that by overthrowing the current Shah, he can pave the way for his father, Rostam, to rule. He leads an army against the Persian forces, demonstrating his exceptional strength and combat skills, quickly establishing himself as a major threat. This invasion sets the stage for the fateful encounter with his father.

keyMyths.title: The Duel with Rostam
keyMyths.description: The climax of Sohrab's story is his duel with Rostam. Unbeknownst to either of them, they are father and son. Rostam, hesitant to reveal his identity, fights Sohrab in a grueling battle. Rostam eventually defeats Sohrab, mortally wounding him. As Sohrab lies dying, he reveals the armband given to him by his mother, revealing his true identity as Rostam's son. Rostam is overcome with grief and remorse, realizing the tragic mistake he has made. This scene is considered one of the most heart-wrenching in Persian literature.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Sohrab and Rostam, providing a detailed account of their lives and tragic encounter.
